parts.homeLangDetails: The most common English negating prefix, inherited from Old English. Directly mirrors German un-, Dutch on-, and relatives across Germanic languages. Turns the meaning of the base word on its head: unhappy, unknown, unforgettable.
Before in time or position. Predict, prepare, prehistoric.

words.translationLong: never done or known before; without precedent
words.exampleSentence: The pandemic caused unprecedented disruption to schools worldwide.
words.exampleTranslation: The pandemic caused unprecedented disruption to schools worldwide.

parts.homeLangDetails: Downward, away, or reversing an action. Decrease, depart, deconstruct. Often directly reverses the base word.
With or together. One of the most productive Latin prefixes. Connect, combine, cooperate, collect. The spelling varies to match the following sound.

The base form 'deconstruct' functions as a verb infinitive. In de-con-struct, the -ed suffix (if present, as in 'deconstructed') marks the past participle; the bare form 'deconstruct' is the infinitive.

words.translationLong: to take apart, to disassemble, to analyze by breaking into components
words.exampleSentence: In this seminar, we will deconstruct the main arguments in the text.
words.exampleTranslation: In this seminar, we will deconstruct the main arguments in the text.
words.flag: base verb form with no overt infinitive suffix
